raw_case:
Lipid Pneumonia Mimicking Lung Cancer in a Middle-Age Woman
Lipid pneumonia is exceedingly rare, with only a few reported cases to date. A 46-year-old woman with a history of left breast cancer underwent a left-modified radical mastectomy, adjuvant chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Despite no known exposure to lipids, she presented with chronic non-productive cough and general malaise. Follow-up chest computed tomography revealed progressive ground-glass opacities in the left lower lung, initially suspected to be lobar bronchioloalveolar carcinoma. Surgical intervention was performed for both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes, confirming the lesion as lipid pneumonia upon pathological examination, revealing the presence of foamy histiocytes.

Introduction

Lipid pneumonia is inflammation of the lungs caused by the accumulation of fatty substances. It is classified into exogenous and endogenous types based on the origin of the lipids. Exogenous lipid pneumonia (ELP) occurs due to inhalation or aspiration of fats, whereas ELP involves the accumulation of lipids within the alveoli. This condition is rare, and its specific pathogenesis remains unclear to date, with only a few documented case reports in humans.

Case Presentation

A 49-year-old woman, employed as a waitress in a restaurant, had a history of left breast cancer (pT2N2M0 stage IIIA). She underwent left modified radical mastectomy at age 38, followed by adjuvant chemotherapy including doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, and docetaxel, as well as regional radiotherapy. Her chest radiography was normal, and she had no history of oil ingestion.Seven years later, she presented with general malaise, and chest roentgenograms revealed increased infiltration in the left lung field. Chest computed tomographic (CT) scan showed regional ground-glass opacity (GGO) in the left lower lung (Fig. 1A). After 1 year, she developed a dry cough and chest discomfort. A repeat chest CT scan indicated progression in the size of GGO in the left lower lung (Fig. 1B and 1C). The initial impression was a malignancy such as lobar bronchioloalveolar carcinoma. Consequently, she underwent a thoracoscopic lobectomy of the left lower lung.Pathological examination revealed infiltration of chronic inflammatory cells and alveolar spaces filled with CD163+ foamy histiocytes and proteinaceous exudates, consistent with lipid pneumonia (Fig. 2).One year post-surgery, the patient remained asymptomatic and underwent a chest CT scan as part of postoperative surveillance, revealing newly developed areas of mixed consolidation and GGO in the right middle lung. We will continue to implement an optimal postoperative surveillance strategy for the patient.

Discussion

Lipid pneumonia is pulmonary inflammation caused by the aspiration or inhalation of oily or fatty substances, first reported by Laughlen in 1925.1) It can be classified into exogenous and endogenous types based on the source of the lipids. ELP occurs due to the inhalation or aspiration of fatty substances. ELP, on the other hand, involves the accumulation of lipids within the alveoli as a consequence of obstruction, chronic lung inflammation, or systemic disease.2)ELP is a rare disease, and its specific pathogenesis remains unclear to date. Patients with ELP typically present with nonspecific symptoms such as cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, or respiratory distress.3) The diagnosis of ELP requires a lung biopsy, which pathologically confirms the presence of lipid-laden macrophages without evidence of external lipid intake. Based on a few case reports of ELP, some studies have reported improvement after administering corticosteroids, while others have shown no change or worsening despite medication.4,5)In pathological sections, a previous study reported the presence of bronchial arthrofibrosis in patients with ELP, which may be a unique characteristic distinguishing it from exogenous lipoid pneumonia.3) However, our patient did not exhibit bronchial arthrofibrosis or interstitial fibrosis.Our case involves an idiopathic ELP patient with a history of left breast cancer treated with mastectomy, adjuvant chemotherapy, and regional radiotherapy. Based on clinical history, physical examination, and chest CT scan findings, the differential diagnosis was initially considered post-irradiation fibrosis or malignancy. However, radiation-induced lung injury was deemed less likely since the affected region did not correspond with the location of the lesion. Due to suspicion of malignancy, the patient underwent lobectomy surgery.Previous case reports utilized fluoroscopy-guided transbronchial forceps or CT-guided biopsy to establish the diagnosis of lipid pneumonia.6,7) However, in our case, the lesion was located distant from the bronchi, rendering transbronchial biopsy impractical. In addition, after discussing with the radiologists, it was determined that a CT-guided core needle biopsy was not available for the patient due to the lesion being subsolid and heterogeneous. Given concerns that tissue obtained from biopsy might not provide adequate diagnostic material, surgery was deemed essential in our case to confirm the diagnosis definitively and to treat potential malignancy.Tracing back to the history of our patient, she received adjuvant chemotherapy, including cyclophosphamide, for breast cancer. According to literature reviews, cyclophosphamide has long been recognized for its potential pulmonary toxicity.8) Moreover, Sulkowski et al. reported that cyclophosphamide-induced lung injury in rats may be linked to the pathogenesis of ELP.9) Their study suggested that pulmonary alveolar proteinosis-like changes resulted from endothelial damage following intraperitoneal cyclophosphamide injection.To our knowledge, our patient represents the first proposed case where cyclophosphamide may be associated with ELP in humans, although the exact mechanism remains incompletely understood.

Conclusion

We present our clinical experience managing a rare case of ELP mimicking lung cancer, successfully treated and diagnosed via thoracoscopic lobectomy. In certain clinical circumstances, CT-guided or transbronchial biopsy could serve as a pre-operative option for confirmatory diagnosis. Given that our patient received cyclophosphamide, an antineoplastic agent, 7 years prior, there may be an association with the pathogenesis of ELP. Further investigation is urgently needed to establish management guidelines for ELP in the future.

Declarations



Ethics approval and consent to participate

Informed consent was obtained from the patient included in the study.

Consent for publication

The participant has consented to the submission of the case report to the journal.

Funding

No funding was provided for the research.

Disclosure statement

Not applicable.

Data availability

No new data were created or analyzed during this study, and data sharing is not applicable to this article.

Author contributions

Jiun-Chang Wu: data collection and draft manuscript.All authors reviewed the results and approved the final version of the manuscript.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 46-year-old woman (waitress).  
- **Chief Complaint**: Chronic non-productive cough and general malaise.  
- **History of Present Illness**: Progressive ground-glass opacities (GGO) identified in the left lower lung over the course of a year, with accompanying dry cough. Initially suspected to be lobar bronchioloalveolar carcinoma.  
- **Past Medical History**: History of left breast cancer (pT2N2M0, stage IIIA); underwent left modified radical mastectomy, adjuvant chemotherapy (doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, and docetaxel), and regional radiotherapy.  
- **Personal History**: No report of oil ingestion or known lipid exposure.  
- **Family History**: None reported.  
- **Physical Examination**: Not detailed in the provided report.  
- **Ancillary Tests**:  
  - **Initial Chest CT**: Revealed ground-glass opacities in the left lower lung.  
  - **Follow-Up Chest CT**: Showed progression of GGO in size over time.  
  - **Pathologic Examination of Lobectomy Specimen**: Displayed chronic inflammatory cell infiltration with alveolar spaces filled with CD163+ foamy histiocytes and proteinaceous exudates, confirming lipid pneumonia.  

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Diagnosis Considerations**: The chief complaint of chronic cough and the CT findings of progressive GGO raised suspicion for malignancy, with lobar bronchioloalveolar carcinoma as the leading diagnosis.  

2. **Differentiation Between Potential Diagnoses**:  
   - **Post-Radiotherapy Fibrosis**: Excluded because the affected lung region did not correspond to radiation exposure sites.  
   - **Malignancy (e.g., bronchioloalveolar carcinoma)**: Strongly considered due to imaging findings and patient's cancer history. Definitive diagnosis required tissue biopsy.  
   - **Lipid Pneumonia**: Not initially suspected due to the absence of a history of lipid exposure or ingestion, but the presence of lipid-laden foamy macrophages on biopsy led to this being conclusively diagnosed.  

3. **Diagnostic Approach**: Given the subsolid and heterogeneous nature of the lesion and the distance from the bronchi, CT-guided and transbronchial biopsies were deemed infeasible. Therefore, surgery was required to both diagnose and treat the lesion.  

4. **Possible Contributing Factors**: Cyclophosphamide, used during the patient's breast cancer treatment, was proposed as a potential contributor to the pathogenesis of lipid pneumonia, supported by preclinical studies.  

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Endogenous lipid pneumonia (ELP), likely associated with prior chemotherapy exposure (cyclophosphamide).

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case step by step. The patient is a 46-year-old woman with a history of left breast cancer, stage IIIA, treated with mastectomy, chemo (including doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, docetaxel), and radiotherapy. Her main issues now are a chronic non-productive cough and general malaise. The imaging shows progressive ground-glass opacities in the left lower lung over a year. Initially, they suspected bronchioloalveolar carcinoma, but the pathology from the lobectomy showed lipid pneumonia.

First, I need to connect her past medical history with the current symptoms. She had breast cancer with treatment that includes chemo and radiation. Chemo drugs can sometimes cause lung issues, like pneumonitis, but that's usually acute. Radiation pneumonitis is a possibility too, especially if the radiation field included the lung. But the timeline here is a year of progression, which might be a bit long for radiation-induced damage, which typically occurs within months. However, delayed radiation effects can happen.

Next, the imaging shows ground-glass opacities (GGOs) that are progressing. The initial suspicion was cancer, like bronchioloalveolar carcinoma (now known as adenocarcinoma in situ), which can present as GGOs. But the pathology came back as lipid pneumonia. Lipid pneumonia is either exogenous (from inhaling or aspirating lipids) or endogenous (idiopathic). The patient has no history of oil ingestion or lipid exposure, so exogenous is less likely. Endogenous lipid pneumonia is associated with conditions like obstruction leading to lipid accumulation, but here the pathology shows foamy histiocytes filled with CD163+ cells and proteinaceous exudates. CD163 is a marker for macrophages, so the histiocytes are macrophages filled with lipids, which fits lipid pneumonia.

But why would she develop lipid pneumonia? Endogenous lipid pneumonia can be due to airway obstruction, leading to lipid accumulation from breakdown of cells. Her history of breast cancer and treatment might lead to lymphatic obstruction or something else causing localized obstruction. Alternatively, maybe the radiotherapy caused some damage leading to impaired clearance. Another angle: chemotherapy agents like taxanes (docetaxel is one) can cause pulmonary toxicity, but more often interstitial pneumonitis. However, the pathology here points to lipid pneumonia, which is different.

The key here is that the pathological diagnosis confirmed lipid pneumonia. The initial suspicion of malignancy was ruled out by biopsy. So the final diagnosis is endogenous lipid pneumonia. The possible contributing factors could be her prior treatments causing some local damage or obstruction, leading to the accumulation of lipid-laden macrophages. But since there's no exogenous source reported, endogenous is the way to go.

Wait, but she had a lobectomy. The GGO was in the left lower lung, which was resected. The pathology from that specimen showed the features of lipid pneumonia. So the final diagnosis based on the pathology is lipid pneumonia. The cause is likely endogenous given no history of exposure. But why did it develop? Maybe post-obstructive due to prior cancer treatment effects? Or maybe radiation-induced changes leading to impaired clearance. However, without evidence of obstruction or aspiration, the cause is endogenous. So the answer should be endogenous lipid pneumonia.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient has a history of left breast cancer treated with mastectomy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Chronic non-productive cough and progressive ground-glass opacities (GGO) in the left lower lung raise concerns for malignancy (e.g., metastatic disease or primary lung cancer) or treatment-related lung injury.  

<step 2> Initial suspicion of lobar bronchioloalveolar carcinoma was ruled out by pathologic examination of the lobectomy specimen, which revealed chronic inflammation, CD163+ foamy histiocytes, and proteinaceous exudates—hallmarks of lipid pneumonia.  

<step 3> Lipid pneumonia can be exogenous (due to aspiration/inhalation of lipids) or endogenous (idiopathic or secondary to airway obstruction). The absence of oil ingestion or lipid exposure excludes exogenous causes.  

<step 4> Endogenous lipid pneumonia may result from alveolar macrophage accumulation of lipids due to disrupted clearance mechanisms. Prior radiotherapy or chemotherapy could contribute to localized tissue damage, impaired lymphatic drainage, or macrophage dysfunction, creating a microenvironment for lipid accumulation.  

<step 5> The pathologic confirmation of lipid pneumonia, combined with the exclusion of malignancy and exogenous lipid sources, solidifies the diagnosis of endogenous lipid pneumonia in the context of prior cancer therapy.  

### Answer:
Endogenous lipid pneumonia (confirmed by histopathology).  
```
